data.title: McPherson's Unit Enters Pharmacy Wholesaler Agreement
data.description:
McPherson's said that its unit, McPherson's Consumer Products, entered into a three-year pharmacy wholesaler agreement with Sigma, a unit of Sigma Healthcare , according to a Monday filing with the Australian bourse.

The agreement, effective in May, will shift McPherson's operations from its current direct-to-store model to a third-party wholesaling model, the filing said.

This partnership will see McPherson's transition an annual revenue stream of AU$50 million to AU$55 million from retailers to Sigma, the filing added.

McPherson's shares were down 2% in recent Monday trade, while Sigma Healthcare's shares gained about 1%.

data.title: Trump says US talking to four different groups on sale of TikTok
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Sunday that his administration was in touch with four different groups about the sale of Chinese-owned social media platform TikTok, and that all options were good.

TikTok's fate has been up in the air since a law requiring its owner ByteDance to either sell it on national security grounds or face a ban took effect on January 19. Trump, after taking office on January 20, signed an executive order seeking to delay by 75 days the enforcement of the law.

Asked if there was going to soon be a deal on TikTok, Trump told reporters aboard the Air Force One, "it could."

"We're dealing with four different groups, and a lot of people want it ... all four are good," he added.

TikTok and ByteDance did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment outside of normal business hours.

The turmoil at TikTok has attracted several potential buyers, including former Los Angeles Dodgers owner Frank McCourt, who have expressed interest in the fast-growing business analysts estimate could be worth as much as $50 billion.

data.title: Market Chatter: Kia Tasman Pickup Truck Surpasses 4,000 Sales in 17 Days
data.description:
South Korean automaker Kia sold over 4,000 units of its first pickup truck, the Tasman, in 17 days since its launch on Feb. 13, boosting expectations for South Korea's pickup truck market, Yonhap News reported Sunday.

The February sales accounted for nearly 30% of the country's total pickup sales last year. The Tasman debuted globally at the 2024 Jeddah International Motor Show in Saudi Arabia, the report said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Utah’s Senate passes Bitcoin bill — but scraps key provision
data.description:
Utah’s Bitcoin bill has passed the state Senate, but without its cornerstone, a clause that would have made it the first US state with its own Bitcoin reserve.

The HB230 “Blockchain and Digital Innovation Amendments” bill now only provides Utah citizens with basic custody protections, the right to mine Bitcoin , run a node and participate in staking, among other things.

The 19-7-3 vote to pass the measure on March 7 means the bill is now headed to Utah Governor Spencer Cox’s desk to be signed into law.

The reserve clause would have authorized Utah’s treasurer to invest up to 5% of digital assets with a market cap above $500 billion over the last calendar year in five state accounts — with Bitcoin as the only digital asset that currently meets this criteria.

The reserve clause passed the second reading but was scrapped in the third and final reading. Utah’s House then concurred with the amendment in a 52-19-4 vote.

“There was a lot of concern with those provisions and the early adoption of these types of policies,” one of the bill’s sponsors, Senator Kirk A. Cullimore, said in Utah’s March 7 floor session.

“All of that has been stripped out of the bill.”

Up until March 7, Utah looked likely to become the first US state to adopt a Bitcoin reserve, Satoshi Action Fund’s CEO Dennis Porter predicted on Feb. 2.

Two Arizona Bitcoin reserve bills and a Texas bill are now the closest to being passed into law, Bitcoin Laws data shows. Each of those bills obtained a successful vote in their respective Senate committees and is now awaiting a final floor vote in the Senate.

Of the 31 Bitcoin reserve state bills introduced, 25 remain live, including bills from Illinois, Iowa, Kentucky, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, New Mexico, North Dakota, Ohio and Oklahoma.

Bills from the likes of Pennsylvania, Montana, Kentucky and North Dakota have failed.

It comes as US President Donald Trump signed an executive order establishing a federal Strategic Bitcoin Reserve on March 7.

The Bitcoin reserve will be seeded with Bitcoin obtained through forfeitures in criminal cases, while the Treasury and Commerce secretaries has been instructed to develop budget-neutral strategies to buy more Bitcoin.

data.title: Boss Energy expands uranium portfolio with Liverpool project earn-in deal
data.description: Boss Energy has entered into an agreement under which it can earn up to a 90% interest in the Liverpool project in the highly prospective Alligator River uranium field in the NT. Boss can earn an initial 49% interest in the project by spending $3 million within three years and a further 31% interest (total 80%) by investing an additional $5m within a further four years. The agreement has been made with the Eclipse Group, comprising Eclipse Metals and its wholly-owned subsidiary North Minerals. Operational control Boss – operator of the Honeymoon project in South Australia and a joint venture (JV) partner in the Alta Mesa project in Texas – will assume operational control of the project during the earn-in stage, with a minimum exploration commitment of $1.5m before it can withdraw from the project. Boss has a further option to purchase an additional 10% interest in the project for $50m upon completion of the earn-in arrangement. Identified as a prospective unconformity-type uranium mineralisation target, the project contains five exploration licences covering an area of 1,229 square kilometres. Previous exploration in the project area has yielded several high-grade uranium assays in rock chips and numerous radiometric anomalies remain untested. Structured agreement Boss managing director Duncan Craib said the agreement had been structured so the company can systematically progress the work with Eclipse through a number of stages and decision points. “Boss has developed some leading uranium exploration technical capacity and we look forward to applying this at the Liverpool project.” Once Boss has earned the initial 49% interest, it will form an unincorporated JV with the Eclipse Group to govern the future exploration and development of the project. ‘Key milestone’ Eclipse Metals executive chair Carl Popal said the signing is a key milestone for the advancement of the Liverpool uranium project. “Boss Energy will accelerate our exploration efforts, bringing us closer to unlocking the full potential of this highly prospective region,” he said. “This strategic alliance allows Eclipse to enhance shareholder value in this long-held asset while sharpening our focus on critical mineral opportunities.”

data.title: Japan Nominal Wage Growth Slows More Than Expected
data.description:
Nominal wages in Japan rose 2.8% year-on-year in January 2025, slowing from a 4.4% gain in December and coming in below market expectations of 3.2%.

January’s figure was also the lowest reading in three months.

Meanwhile, inflation-adjusted real wages, which reflect consumer purchasing power, fell 1.8% in January from a year ago, ending two straight months of gains as inflation outpaced wage growth.

The report came just days ahead of the culmination of the annual round of pay negotiations at the country’s major firms. Last week, Japan’s largest labor group said member unions were demanding an average 6.09% pay hike, their largest claim in more than 30 years.

On the monetary policy front, the Bank of Japan is expected to hold interest rates steady at its March policy meeting as officials wait for more evidence of sustainable wage growth.

data.title: Market Chatter: South Korean Steelmakers Brace for US Tariffs
data.description:
South Korean steelmakers—Posco and Hyundai Steel —plan to work with the government and industry bodies to mitigate the impact of the US' 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum imports from March 12, Yonhap News reported Friday.

Posco is considering building a US steel plant as a potential response to the tariffs, while Hyundai Steel warns of long-term industry repercussions.

SeAH Steel , which shipped a quarter of its steel pipe production to the US in 2024, also expects business disruptions, the report said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Star Entertainment Receives AU$940 Million Refinancing Proposal, Discloses Other Potential Liquidity Lifeline
data.description:
Star Entertainment Group disclosed various liquidity initiatives amid efforts to stay afloat, including a refinancing proposal that could generate up to AU$940 million, according to a March 7 filing with the Australian bourse.

The cash-strapped casino operator entered into documentation for a senior secured AU$250 million bridge facility with funds managed by King Street Capital Management. The loan is subject to an intercreditor agreement with Star Entertainment's existing lenders, probity approvals, and regulatory consents for the company to provide first ranking security over The Star Gold Coast, among other conditions. The bridge facility is available to be drawn until April 29, per the filing.

Separately, Star Entertainment received a refinancing proposal from an additional potential lender that could provide up to AU$940 million. The undisclosed lender is already engaged in preliminary discussions with governments and regulators, and is under an exclusivity and process deed with the casino operator, the filing stated.

Meanwhile, Star Entertainment said the debt financing proposal from Oaktree Capital Management has not been finalized as certain conditions remain pending.

In the same filing, the company said it received AU$35 million from Chow Tai Fook Enterprises and Far East Consortium International in relation to the The Star Gold Coast joint venture assets. Star Entertainment expects to receive AU$10 million before the end of March and a further AU$8 million once the Andaz hotel is completed or on Nov. 30, whichever occurs earlier.

data.title: South Korea's Kospi Opens 0.3% Lower at 2555.40
Nikkei Stock Average Opens 0.2% Higher at 36972.59
Legacy Iron Ore Executes 'Right to Mine' Deal for Western Australia Gold Project
data.description:
Legacy Iron Ore executed a binding "right to mine" agreement with Bain Global Resources for mining operations at the Mt. Celia gold project in Western Australia, according to a Monday Australian bourse filing.

Bain Global Resources will fund all the mining activities up to AU$10 million under a deferred payment model. It will prepare and transport the stockpiles of ore to the mill and receive a fixed monthly payment of AU$300,000 after the first three months of operations until the project reaches breakeven.

The remaining surplus after Legacy's fixed payment will be allocated to Bain Global until it recovers its initial investment.

Net profits from mining activities will be shared between Legacy and Bain Global in a 75-to-25 ratio until Legacy recovers AU$20 million in profit. On reaching that milestone, net profits will be distributed equally between the two firms.

data.title: Treasure Factory Reports 19% Jump in February Sales
data.description:
Treasure Factory reported a 19.1% year-over-year increase in net sales across all stores for February.

Sales at existing stores grew 9.9% year on year, according to a delayed filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

During the month, winter apparel and heating appliance sales rose amid a lower-temperature environment.

Additionally, brand items, hobby-related items, and sports and outdoor items performed well.

Treasure Factory also opened one store in Odawara and closed one in Nagareyama, bringing the total number of stores to 210.

data.title: Seven & I Holdings: Director Joseph Michael DePinto Steps Down
US stock futures sink, yen gains on China deflationary pressures, trade war
data.description:
Wall Street futures sank and the safe-haven yen strengthened early on Monday as building deflationary pressures in China added to growth worries from a fading U.S. economy and a simmering global trade war.

U.S. S&P 500 e-mini stock futures pointed 0.7% lower and Nasdaq futures sagged 0.9%.

The yen gained about 0.4% to 147.395 per U.S. dollar.

Cryptocurrency bitcoin lost as much as 7.2% from Friday to reach the lowest this month at $80,085.42.

Data on Sunday showed China's consumer price index fell at the sharpest pace in 13 months in February, while producer price deflation extended to a 30th straight month.

Friday's U.S. figures showed the labour market created fewer jobs than expected last month in the first monthly payrolls report capturing President Donald Trump's policies. That extended a recent run of soft readings for the world's biggest economy.

Meanwhile, Trump in a Fox News interview on Sunday declined to predict whether his tariffs on Canada, Mexico and China would result in a U.S. recession.

"There's no shortage of possible catalysts" for early weakness in markets, said Kyle Rodda, senior financial markets analyst at Capital.com.

"Most of all though, I think it's Trump's cavalier approach to economic policy that's rattling sentiment," Rodda said.

"Unlike during his first administration, where signs of an economic slowdown or market correction would see a pivot on policy, he is genuinely focused on significant, structural change to the economy - even if it comes at the expense of short-term growth."

data.title: Japan Records First Current Account Deficit in Two Years
data.description: Japan’s current account turned to a deficit of JPY 256.6 billion in January 2025 from a surplus of JPY 334.3 billion, exceeding market forecasts for a shortfall of JPY 230.5 billion and marking the first gap since January 2023.

data.title: Australia's Goodman rises; Morningstar recommends not to participate in share purchase plan
data.description:
** Shares of Australia's Goodman Group rise 1.1% to A$30.87

** Analysts at Morningstar recommend investors to not participate in the developer's share purchase plan

** Goodman, after completing an A$4 bln ($2.52 bln) placement, is raising A$400 mln via a share purchase plan

** Brokerage feels GMG shares are overvalued and questions the maintainability of excessive returns in data centre investments in the long run

** Competition in the data center space is intensifying, as many rival REITs and data center operators are rapidly adding new supply, and suitable locations will be harder to obtain, says Morningstar

** Stock down 13.9% YTD

($1 = 1.5873 Australian dollars)

data.title: Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Inflation Data, Bank of Canada Decision Due
data.description:
U.S. inflation data for February will be the highlight among economic data as investors continue to follow the back-and-forth of U.S. President Trump's announcements on tariffs.

A rate decision by the Bank of Canada is due, where rates are likely to be cut amid concern about the economic impact of U.S. tariffs on Canadian imports.

In Asia, the focus is on China's annual legislature session, which will end during the week, as well as a slew of data releases from Japan.

U.S.

As more headlines from President Trump on tariffs and Ukraine will continue to be closely monitored, Wednesday's U.S. inflation data for February will be the highlight of the week's economic data.

The U.S. Federal Reserve has raised concerns recently that inflation isn't slowing as much as it had hoped. The data could continue to reflect this, particularly given evidence that the prospect of tariffs has already been feeding through into higher prices.

Business surveys suggest that some companies are pre-empting tariffs by lifting prices, ING economists said in a note. Food prices have also been rising, while energy prices could additionally contribute to more inflation, they said.

Still, the impact of the data on U.S. rate expectations could be limited because of growing concerns about the impact of Trump's policies, especially tariffs, on the economy.

"The market appears more focused on worries about growth right now in an environment of government austerity and the threat of a squeeze on spending power if prices do respond to tariffs," ING said.

U.S. money markets are now pricing in three 25 basis-point rate cuts from the U.S. Federal Reserve this year, versus just one only a few weeks ago.

"A 0.3% month-on-month inflation print is unlikely to change that situation."

With doubts starting to grow about the strength of the U.S. economy, the University of Michigan March preliminary consumer survey on Friday, March 14 could also be significant for gauging sentiment amid tariff uncertainty and public-sector job losses.

Other data include February producer price data and weekly jobless claims figures on Thursday.

On tariffs, the U.S. is set to impose steel and aluminium tariffs of 25% on the EU from Wednesday.

The Treasury will auction $58 billion in three-year notes on Tuesday, $39 billion in 10-year notes on Wednesday and $22 billion in 30-year bonds on Thursday.

CANADA

Canada is one of U.S. President Trump's key targets for tariffs, putting Canadian data and events more in the spotlight than usual.

The Bank of Canada announces a rate decision on Wednesday. With Canada's jobs data for February coming in weak, even before tariffs were implemented, a rate cut looks increasingly likely.

"The slowdown in hiring even before tariffs were implemented gives the BOC an easy pass to cut interest rates another quarter point," economists at Desjardins Group said in a note.

Money markets are pricing in a 78% chance of a rate cut at the upcoming meeting, LSEG data show.

EUROZONE

The data calendar in the coming week is slim, leaving investors continuing to ponder the implications of Germany's large-scale fiscal stimulus plans--which caused a steep rise in German government-bond yields--as well as a planned step-up in defense spending on the continent.

Investors are particularly interested in how the fiscal expansion will impact Europe's growth and inflation, and how that could impact the European Central Bank's monetary policy.

Vanguard and AllianceBernstein already expect fewer ECB rate cuts after this month's 25-basis-point reduction in the deposit rate to 2.50%. Vanguard now expects only one more rate cut this year, while AllianceBernstein's revised baseline for the ECB's policy rate at the end of 2025 is 2%, up from a previous expectation of 1.75%.

Industrial production data from Germany for January are due Monday, followed by eurozone industrial production figures Thursday. Final February inflation data from France and Spain are due on Friday, March 14.

Germany will auction 4.5 billion euros in March 2027 Schatz on Tuesday and 4.5 billion euros in February 2035 Bund on Wednesday. Portugal holds an auction on Wednesday and Italy on Thursday.

U.K.

U.K. gross domestic product data for January are due Friday, March 14 and come after unexpectedly strong GDP for December.

Investec economist Philip Shaw said the data could continue to be solid, but there are many uncertainties ahead which could weigh on growth going forward.

"We are forecasting a 0.2% increase in GDP on the month, but would emphasise that this represents a brisker pace of expansion than is likely over the remainder of the year," he said.

The U.K. plans to launch a new September 2049 index-linked gilt via syndication in the week starting on March 10, subject to demand and market conditions. The U.K. plans the sale of March 2035 gilt Wednesday.

POLAND

Poland's central bank announces a rate decision on Wednesday. It is expected to leave its key interest rate on hold at 5.75% due to continued concerns about inflation.

SCANDINAVIA, SWITZERLAND

Sweden will hold a bond auction on Wednesday. Switzerland will hold a bond auction on Wednesday.

CHINA

China's annual legislature session will end Tuesday when lawmakers are expected to approve a government work report that includes an ambitious 2025 growth target of around 5% for the world's second-largest economy.

Attention is on whether Beijing will follow up on stimulus pledges with more concrete measures, with senior officials having announced plans to step up fiscal spending and boost consumption.

Next week, China's central bank will likely release its gauges of the country's credit growth for February. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal estimated that banks issued 1.23 trillion yuan of new loans last month, a significant fall from January's 5.13 trillion yuan, mainly due to seasonality. Meanwhile, M2, a broad gauge of money supply, may have expanded 7% in February from a year earlier, matching the pace seen in January.

JAPAN

The Japanese Trade Union Confederation, known as Rengo, is scheduled to announce preliminary results for annual spring wage negotiations on Friday, March 14. Japan's largest trade union group has said its member unions are seeking an average wage increase of 6.09%, the biggest request in three decades. These wage negotiations are closely watched by economists and investors for clues on the timing of future Bank of Japan interest-rate increases.

Revised gross domestic product data, scheduled to be released Tuesday, is likely to confirm that the Japanese economy expanded for the third-straight quarter during the October-December period. The preliminary report released in February showed that real GDP grew an annualized 2.8% in the final quarter of 2024.

Separate government data due Tuesday may also signal a recovery in private consumption. Household spending is expected to have increased 3.5% in January from a year earlier, according to a poll of economists by data provider Quick. That compares with the 2.7% rise seen in December.

Figures on current account balance for January and bank lending for February are also slated for release Monday.

The Ministry of Finance is scheduled to auction 2.3 trillion yen of five-year sovereign notes on Monday and 1 trillion yen of 20-year JGBs on Wednesday. The 20-year JGBs to be auctioned will be a reopening of the January 2025 issue, the ministry said. Investors such as insurance companies and pension funds are likely to show more bidding interest at the 20-year auction owing to the issue's higher yields that will probably be available.

AUSTRALIA

On Tuesday, National Australia Bank's business survey for February will attract some attention as markets weigh whether global events might be starting to dent confidence.

Australia's economy has been supported by government spending over the last year, with private demand subdued. The NAB survey may offer some clues on a private demand recovery, which will help to offset an expected slide in public demand later this year.

INDIA

India's February inflation print on Wednesday will likely show a further cooling in price pressures, which could pave the way for further monetary policy easing.

Bank of America economist Rahul Bajoria forecasts headline inflation to have eased to 4.0%, the midpoint of the central bank's target range, which would mark the slowest price growth in six months. Food and vegetable prices likely eased during the month, Bajoria said.

The Reserve Bank of India sees food inflation pressures softening in the coming months, but has warned that continued volatility in energy prices and adverse weather conditions pose upside risks to prices.

Given recent data showing a slowdown in growth and easing inflation, ANZ's Dhiraj Nim and Sanjay Mathur see a "case for a stronger policy response" by the central bank. The economists said in a note that the RBI could do more to stimulate the economy, and expect the RBI to deliver at least two more rate cuts by June. ANZ pegs India's inflation at 3.8% for February.

  • Any references to days are in local times.

Write to Jessica Fleetham at jessica.fleetham@wsj.com and Hoi Shan Chan at hoishan.chan@wsj.com

data.title: Gold Edges Higher, Supported by U.S. Recession Worries — Market Talk
data.description: Gold edges higher in the early morning Asian session, supported by prospects of a U.S. recession that could enhance the safe-haven appeal of the precious metal. In an interview with Fox News on Sunday, President Trump refused to rule out a recession for the U.S. this year. "Traders and investors are reacting to a sharp and sudden deterioration in U.S. economic data," Saxo Bank's Ole S. Hansen says in a research report. Gold's outlook is still supportive, especially given the limited scope of its latest correction, which signals strong demand despite selling pressure from technically focused traders, the head of commodity strategy says. Spot gold is 0.2% higher at $2,915.98/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Natural gas Hits 26-month High
data.description:
Natural gas increased to a 26-month high of 4.64 USD/MMBtu.

Over the past 4 weeks, Natural gas gained 35.17%, and in the last 12 months, it increased 150.01%.

data.title: Nikkei May Rise as Concerns About U.S. Tariffs, Borrowing Costs Ebb — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks may rise as concerns about U.S. tariffs and higher borrowing costs ebb for now. Nikkei futures are up 0.5% at 36985 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 147.52, compared with 147.56 as of Friday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. trade policy as well as Japanese monetary policy. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 2.2% to 36887.17 on Friday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

data.title: Sparx Group's Assets Under Management Slip in February
data.description:
Sparx Group reported assets under management of 1.886 trillion yen at the end of February, down 42.8 billion yen or 2.2% from the previous month, according to a Friday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

By segment, Japan equity assets fell to 1.299 trillion yen, while OneAsia dropped to 109.5 billion yen. Real assets increased to 302.1 billion yen, and private equity slipped to 175.7 billion yen.

data.title: Nikkei May Rise as Concerns About U.S. Tariffs, Borrowing Costs Ebb — Market Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks may rise as concerns about U.S. tariffs and higher borrowing costs ebb for now. Nikkei futures are up 0.5% at 36985 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 147.52, compared with 147.56 as of Friday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. trade policy as well as Japanese monetary policy. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 2.2% to 36887.17 on Friday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

data.title: ASX Small-Cap Stocks to Watch
data.description:
Investors are likely to keep an eye on the following small-cap stocks listed on the Australian Securities Exchange.

Argosy Minerals

Argosy Minerals signed a spot sales contract with a Hong Kong-based chemical company for the sale of 20 metric tonnes of battery-quality lithium carbonate product from its Rincon project in Argentina.

New Frontier Minerals

New Frontier Minerals agreed to sell all of the shares in its subsidiary, BHA No. 1, to Impact Minerals . BHA holds the Broken Hill East project in New South Wales.

Brazilian Critical Minerals

Brazilian Critical Minerals received results from an Australian Nuclear Science and Technology Organization column test, which replicated conditions from in-situ samples at the Ema project in Brazil. A head grade analysis of the sample was determined to contain 829 parts per million of 15 rare earth elements and yttrium, of which 30% were neodymium, praseodymium, dysprosium, and terbium.

Advance Metals

Advance Metals named Jay Stephenson as chief financial officer and will succeed CFO Wayne Kernaghan following the latter's resignation.

Olympio Metals

Olympio Metals received final approval to drill for the Dufay project in Canada. Contractor Forage Val d'Or is engaged for the planned 1,500-meter drilling. The rig is set to mobilize to the site next week.

data.title: Australia's Harris Technology rises on premium placement
data.description:
** Shares of Harris Technology Group rise 60% to A$0.016

** Stock marks its largest intraday pct jump since early August 2020

** The online retailing firm agrees to a private placement of shares to Taiwan-based tech co FSP Technology Inc to raise A$1.494 million ($941,817.60)

** Issue price at A$0.02 per share, double the stock's last close of A$0.01

** Funds to be used to drive co's expansion into product segments like pre-owned electronics

** About 2.5 mln shares change hands, 8.2 times the 30-day avg

** Stock up 60% YTD, including current session's moves

($1 = 1.5863 Australian dollars)

data.title: One REIT Raises Financial Forecast for Fiscal Ending August
data.description:
One REIT has raised its financial forecast and cash distribution for the fiscal period ending August due to the acquisition of Comfort Inn Nagoya Sakae, leading to increased property-related revenue, according to its filing last Friday.

The new projections show an increase in operating revenue to 5.25 billion yen from 5.08 billion yen and a rise in net income to 2.38 billion yen from 2.28 billion yen.

The distribution per unit is also expected to grow to 8,000 yen from 7,640 yen.

data.title: Dai-ichi Life's Protective Life Unit to Cede $9.7 Billion Insurance Block to Resolution Life
data.description:
Dai-ichi Life Holdings said its US subsidiary Protective Life Corporation will cede about $9.7 billion in policy reserves from universal life and structured settlement contracts to Resolution Life, according to a Friday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The deal, set to close in phases through October, is expected to improve Dai-ichi's adjusted profit by $30 million-$40 million annually and raise its ESR by around two percentage points.

The transaction will not involve asset transfers, but future revenue and expenses from the ceded contracts will shift to Resolution Life.

data.title: Medical Data Vision Secures SBT Certification for Emissions Reduction Targets
data.description:
Medical Data Vision received Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) certification for its near-term greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction goals, aligning with the Paris Agreement's 1.5°C target, according to a Friday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The health data company aims to cut Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 42% and Scope 3 emissions by 25% from FY2023 levels by FY2030.

Medical Data Vision plans to use eco-friendly data centers and transition to renewable energy to meet these targets.

data.title: Atturra's Unit Acquires US-Based Cloud Integration, Services Firm
data.description:
Atturra has acquired US-based Kitepipe, through its unit, Atturra North America, according to a Monday filing with the Australian bourse.

The deal, finalized under a membership interest purchase agreement, includes an upfront cash payment of $3.15 million and potential earn-out payments of up to $3.18 million based on Kitepipe achieving performance targets for the current fiscal year and the fiscal year 2026, the filing said.

Integration costs for the deal are estimated at $350,000, the filing added.
